coredata.dc:title: Reaction Temperature-Controlled synthesis of multicolor aggregation-induced emission carbon dots and their various applications in anti-counterfeiting, fingerprint detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Liming
Zhang, Cheng
Li, Haitao
Jian, Ke
Han, Yunhai
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Although carbon dots (CDs) are a fluorescent nanomaterial with tremendous development potential, solid-state CDs have not been effectively explored due to the fluorescence quenching effect caused by aggregation. Herein, multi-color tunable aggregation-induced emission (AIE) CDs were constructed using dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) and ammonium sulfate as raw materials by controlling the reaction temperature. Herein, the AIE behavior of CDs is mainly ascribed to the presence of disulfide bonds, which not only enhance the hydrophobicity of CDs, but also restrict the intramolecular rotation of the aggregated CDs, thus improving the surface state fluorescence of CDs, resulting in solid-state fluorescence (SSF) in longwave emission. More importantly, the emission behavior of the obtained CDs can be regulated by controlling the reaction temperature, which may be due to the synergistic effect caused by the differences in N, S content, functional group ratio, and size distribution of synthesized CDs at different temperatures, meanwhile producing different AIE behavior and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) efficiency, leading to the corresponding five different colors of AIE CDs. Furthermore, the synthesized AIE CDs have been successfully employed in various applications, including discoloration hydrogel, color-changing anti-counterfeit paper, and efficient latent fingerprints detection with enhanced details.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission
Tunable multi-color emission
Discoloration hydrogel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the fluorescence color-changing gel
Optical properties of CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220
Characterization of CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CD120–CD220. (b-f) UV–vis absorption, optimal PL excitation and emission spectra of CD120–CD220. (g-k) PL emission spectra of CD120–CD220 at different excitation wavelengths. (l-p) Excitation–emission matrix for the CD120–CD220.

(a) Images of CD220 dissolved in water/DMSO mixture solution under visible and UV light, respectively, with varying volume ratios of water (0 %-90 %). (b) Photographs of CD220 solutions with water/DMSO ratios of 30 %, 40 %, and 70 % under bright field and UV light conditions in an upright fluorescence microscope. (c) Images of CD120-CD220 dissolved in DMSO with different concentrations (0.5, 2.5, 5, 20, 40 mg/mL) under UV light. (d-h) The normalized PL spectra of CD120-CD220 dissolved in DMSO at different concentrations. (i) Spectral overlap of the absorption spectra of the concentrated solution (40 mg/mL) with the emission spectra of the diluted solution (0.5 mg/mL) (j) Schematic of AIE effect of CDs.

(a-b) TEM, HR-TEM images and particle size distribution histograms of CD140 and CD220, respectively. (c, e, g, i, k) XPS spectra of CD120–CD220. (d, f, h, j, l) XPS high-resolution N 1 s spectra of CD120–CD220, respectively. (m & n) XRD and FT-IR spectra of CD120–CD220, respectively.

(a) Time-dependent photographs of CD220 color-changing hydrogel immersed in water and subsequently placed in ethanol. (b) Schematic diagram of color change mechanism of hydrogel placed in water. (c) The aggregation-induced color change process of CD220′s chromogenic gel under upright fluorescence microscopy. (d) Maple leaves sprayed with CD120-CD220 ink. The photographs were taken under 365 nm UV light. (e) The rose, rabbit, and five-petal flower models, fabricated using color-changing CDs ink, were immersed in ethanol (top) and water (bottom), respectively, and the photographs were taken under 365 nm UV light.

(a) Photographs of the CD180, CD200 and CD220 powder-stained latent fingerprints on glass substrates under sunlight and UV light irradiation, respectively. Images for (1–12): magnified images of details. (b) The potential fingerprint images of CD220, stained with powder on surfaces glass, mobile phone case, plastic and iron, were captured under sunlight and ultraviolet light, respectively.

head.ce:title: Reaction Temperature-Controlled synthesis of multicolor aggregation-induced emission carbon dots and their various applications in anti-counterfeiting, fingerprint detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Applied Technology, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Applied Technology, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-color and tunable AIE CDs were constructed by solvothermal method and applied in color-changing gel, anti-counterfeiting technology and fingerprint detection.

Although carbon dots (CDs) are a fluorescent nanomaterial with tremendous development potential, solid-state CDs have not been effectively explored due to the fluorescence quenching effect caused by aggregation. Herein, multi-color tunable aggregation-induced emission (AIE) CDs were constructed using dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) and ammonium sulfate as raw materials by controlling the reaction temperature. Herein, the AIE behavior of CDs is mainly ascribed to the presence of disulfide bonds, which not only enhance the hydrophobicity of CDs, but also restrict the intramolecular rotation of the aggregated CDs, thus improving the surface state fluorescence of CDs, resulting in solid-state fluorescence (SSF) in longwave emission. More importantly, the emission behavior of the obtained CDs can be regulated by controlling the reaction temperature, which may be due to the synergistic effect caused by the differences in N, S content, functional group ratio, and size distribution of synthesized CDs at different temperatures, meanwhile producing different AIE behavior and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) efficiency, leading to the corresponding five different colors of AIE CDs. Furthermore, the synthesized AIE CDs have been successfully employed in various applications, including discoloration hydrogel, color-changing anti-counterfeit paper, and efficient latent fingerprints detection with enhanced details.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, possess adjustable optical properties, easy preparation and surface modification, and excellent biocompatibility [1–4]. Therefore, they have extensive applications in fields such as sensing, bioimaging, cancer therapy, and luminescent devices [5–8]. However, the development and application of CDs have been impeded by aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) resulting from excessive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or π-π stacking [9–12]. To address this issue, many researchers have incorporated CDs into solid substrates to attain powerful solid-state fluorescence emission [13–17]. However, this approach still faces some challenges, as polymers and matrices not only increase fabrication costs, but may also impact the performance of CDs. To overcome these problems, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) CDs have garnered increasing attention, and several rational strategies have been proposed to generate and enhance their fluorescence in the aggregated state. Nonetheless, achieving tunable multi-color emission from CDs seems challenging due to the unclear origins of the emission in CDs. Preliminary studies suggest that the photoluminescence (PL) of CDs may arise from the “external” emission of functional groups such as CO, CN, and others [18,19]. Consequently, doping with electron-donating N can generate new states and absorption domains through n-π* transitions of C = N. Additionally, unlike conventionally prepared water-soluble CDs, AIE CDs exhibit good hydrophobicity, and their solid-state fluorescence emission differs significantly from their solution state [20,21]. So far, only a few research groups have been able to successfully prepare AIE CDs with tunable multicolor emission. Hu’s group successfully synthesized AIE CDs emitting fluorescence from green to red by solvent-thermal treating DTSA and amines with varying numbers of nitrogen atoms [22]. Recently, Lu’s group achieved the preparation of a series of full-color luminescent AIE CDs by modifying the previously synthesized CDs with different salicylaldehyde ligands [23]. Nonetheless, these CDs require time-consuming post-treatments and different carbon sources alter their emission properties, hindering the analysis of their emission mechanism [24]. Ding's group prepared a new type of multi-color AIE-CD by a single pot solvothermal method using salicylic acid, thiosalicylic acid, and 2,2′- dithiodibenzoic acid [25]. Kou et al. synthesized a novel yellow fluorescent rare earth doped AIE-CD using TPE, Yb (NO3)3 and Er (NO3)3 by a one pot hydrothermal method [26]. Hu et al. synthesized AIE-CD using one-step microwave method using phloroglucinol, L-cysteine, and boric acid [27]. Therefore, developing a simple and effective one-step method to produce high-performance full-color solid-state fluorescent AIE CDs using the same precursor remains a major challenge. This is of significant importance for the synthesis and practical application of solid-state fluorescence CDs, as well as for gaining a fundamental understanding of their emission mechanism [28].

Previously, our group prepared multi-color CDs using DTSA as the carbon source, urea, sulfonamide and melamine as the nitrogen sources [29]. In this work, DTSA still served as the carbon source, instead, inorganic salts (ammonium sulfate) were used as the nitrogen source, and constructed multi-color, tunable AIE CDs by controlling the solvothermal temperature (120–220 ℃). In the dispersed state, the prepared AIE CDs all exhibit the similar blue emission from the conjugated π − domain in the carbon nucleus, while surface fluorophores containing S-S bonds do not emit fluorescence due to the intramolecular rotation of S-S bonds. In contrast, in the aggregated state, the carbon core luminescent center is partially subjected to ACQ due to its larger conjugated structure, resulting in the shutdown of short-wavelength fluorescence emission, while the surface fluorescent groups containing S-S bonds are limited by the aggregation of CDs, displaying exhibit long-wavelength fluorescence emission similar to the AIE effect [20,30]. Additionally, the reaction temperature plays a regulatory role in the nitrogen content of CDs, providing an attempt to modify the emission characteristics of CDs. The synthesis of five-color AIE CDs demonstrated the simplicity and feasibility of our approach. Furthermore, the applications of the synthesized AIE CDs in color-changing gels, anti-counterfeiting technology and fingerprint detection were explored.

DTSA was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonium sulfate and Acetic acid were purchased from Beijing chemical reagents factory (Beijing, China). All reagents are analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fluorescence (FL) spectra were obtained with the Fluoromax-4 fluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured with the TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing General Analytical Instruments Co, Beijing, China). Fluorescence decay curves and quantum yield (QY) were obtained with the Edinburg FLS1000 steady state and time-resolved photoluminescence spectrometer. Fluorescence photographs were obtained using the fluorescence microscope (Eclipse Ni-U, Nikon, Kyoto, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained with the JSM-2100PLUS microscope (JOEL, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were carried out on the Smart Lab 3 kW X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) and 2θ range of 5–80°. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained by KBr pelletmethod on the Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Waltham, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed on the ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA).

First, 0.25 g of DTSA and ammonium sulfate (0.1 g) were dissolved in acetic acid solution (10 mL). Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon reactor and placed it in a muffle furnace for further carbonization reaction at different temperature for 24 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was poured into 500 mL of boiling water, and then filtered and dried to obtain AIE CDs powder. Reaction temperatures was set up at 120 ℃, 140 ℃, 180 ℃, 200 ℃ and 220 ℃, respectively and the corresponding prepared AIE CDs were named CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescence color-changing gel
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 0.04 g of AIE CDs power was dissolved in a 20 mL solution of DMSO. Subsequently, 0.8 g of gelatin was gradually added to the solution, and stirred until the gelatin was completely dissolved. The obtained mixed solution was dropped into water to fabricate a fluorescence color-changing gel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of AIE CDs involved solvent thermal treatment at different temperatures with DTSA as precursor, (NH4)2SO4 as nitrogen source and acetic acid as solvent, followed by purifying by adding boiling water, accompanied with the aggregation-induced precipitation process (Fig. 1 a). It must be noted that DTSA possesses a symmetric molecular structure with unique S-S bond, which is a crucial part of the synthesized AIE CDs with solid-state fluorescence. Additionally, acetic acid not only serves as a catalyst for the carbonization process and plays a crucial role in the formation of CDs, but also affects the surface composition of CDs through synergistic effects [29]. Ultimately, multi-color solid-state fluorescence CDs from blue to red were obtained by controlling the reaction temperatures from 120℃ to 220℃, and the corresponding prepared AIE CDs were named CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220, respectively.

Furthermore, from Fig. 1b–f and Fig. S1, it can be observed that the CDs powder exhibits pronounced UV–Vis absorption characteristics in the wavelength range of 200–600 nm, which is closely related to their PL emission. The CDs show strong absorption from 200-350 nm. These peaks are ascribed to the π–π* transition of aromatic bonds (C = C and C–C) from the carbon core and then–π* transition of C-O and C–N bonds in conjugated sp2domains, respectively [31]. Particularly, CD120 did not show obvious peaks in the visible region wavelength longer than 350 nm, which is consistent with previous reports [13]. However, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220 exhibit gradually prominent peak at about 450 and 560 nm, which can commonly be assigned to the surface-state transition (C = O, C = N, C–O, and C–S) of CDs, further causing the emission color change from blue (CD120) to cyan (CD140), green (CD180), yellow (CD200) and red (CD220) [32]. In addition, it can be found that the excitation spectra of the five samples overlapped more and more with their corresponding low-energy absorption bands, which contributed to the emission of blue, cyan, green, yellow, and red.

The PL emission spectra exhibit systematic variations (Fig. 1g–k), with an increasing trend of long-wavelength emission relative to short-wavelength emission. The optimal emission of CD120-CD220 shifts towards longer wavelengths, located at 450, 475, 485, 590 and 615 nm for CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220, respectively (Fig. S2). CIE coordinates of CD120-CD220 further indicated the color transitions from blue to red from CD120 to CD220 (Fig. S3). Meanwhile, it is not difficult to observe that, CD120 and CD140 display single excitation and emission center, while dual excitation and emission center for CD180, CD200 and CD220 (Fig. 1l–p), accompanied with red-shift. Notably, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength of the CDs solution (Fig. S4) are around 360 nm and 450 nm, respectively, exhibiting blue PL characteristics. The fluorescence of CDs in solution is attributed to the π-π* transitions and the presence of a large conjugated structure, which are characteristic of carbon core fluorescence in typical CDs [20]. Remarkably, they exhibit short-wavelength fluorescence emission in the dispersed state, but shift towards long-wavelength in the aggregated state. We refer to this phenomenon as AIE transformation emission, where the AIE behavior alters the emission properties of the CDs [29].

Time-resolved PL decay curves of CD120-CD220 were further investigated (Fig. S5). The measured decay curves were accurately fitted using a double exponential function I(t) (Eq. (1)), which has two lifetimes, τ1 and τ2, and then the corresponding average fluorescence lifetime(τave) was calculated as followed Eq. (2).
(1)
I
t
=
B 1
exp
-
t
τ 1
+
B 2
exp
-
t
τ 2
(2)
τ
ave
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ
2
2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2

Here, τ1 and τ2 are the decay time constants, B1 and B2 represent the pre-factors for time-resolved decay lifetime of τ1 and τ2, and A1(%) and A2(%) are the lifetime-weighted fractional intensities for each component obtained by weighted calculation of B1 and B2, respectively, representing the intensity contributions of each component in the double exponential fitting. The fitted data for the fluorescence lifetimes of CD120-CD220 are shown in Table S1, with τave values of 4.582 ns, 2.413 ns, 2.296 ns, 5.629 ns and 5.154 ns, respectively. The spectral overlap between the emission of graphitized nuclei and the absorption of heteroatom clusters enriched on their surfaces allows energy to transfer from the nucleus to the surface of the heteroatom clusters. Indeed, this energy transfer phenomenon occurs through direct π − π interactions or resonant energy transfer (RET), leading to a shortened fluorescence lifetime [29]. A shorter fluorescence lifetime indicates a greater intensity of fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was determined and found to be 2.32 %, 1.46 %, 0.58 %, 8.01 %, and 1.71 % for CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220, respectively. It is worth noting that these relatively low values suggest a correlation between fluorescence and their high photon absorption [22]. Furthermore, the radiative decay rate (KR) and non-radiative decay rate (KNR) of CD120-CD220 in the solid state were calculated using equations Eq. (3) and Eq. (4).
(3)
K R
= Q Y / τ
(4)
QY =
K R
/
(
K R
+
K
NR
)

The calculated values of KR and KNR are shown in Table S2, where the non-radiative decay rate (KNR) is relatively low in the solid state, while KR shows no significant variation. Therefore, CD120-CD220 follow the fundamental principles associated with AIE activity [22].

Furthermore, the aggregation-induced process of CD120-CD220 was investigated in detail. Interestingly, CD120-CD220 are stable and dissolve in DMSO but are insoluble in water. When the volume of water is less than 30 %, these CDs solutions appear transparent under sunlight and emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. With an increasing volume of water, as water volume up to 40 %, these homogeneous CDs solutions become turbid, accompanied by absorption and emission enhancement and red-shift to a certain extent. These aggregated CDs nanoparticles can precipitate at the bottom of the solution after standing and emit strong fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the typical CD220 display the competition between the red emission from the observed the turbid solutions of CD220 with water contents of 30 %, 40 %, and 70 % under an upright fluorescence microscope (Fig. 2b), revealing the gradual formation of aggregated fluorescent particles with increasing water volume, further proving the aggregation behavior of CDs induced by water. This phenomenon is consistent with the widely recognized and accepted AIE concept [21,33–35]. Remarkably, CD120-CD200 exhibit similar AIE behavior, except for the fluorescence emission color in aggregated state (Fig. S6 & S7). It is noteworthy that with the increase in reaction temperature, CDs tend to aggregate in water and exhibit a more prominent AIE phenomenon. This may be due to the decrease of hydrophilic groups on the CD surface caused by the elevated temperature.

Subsequently, the effect of CDs concentration on their optical properties was investigated to further elucidated the AIE redshift mechanism of these CDs. As shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d–h, it is evident that CD120-CD220 solutions display concentration-dependent optical properties, and the corresponding maximum PL emission peaks of CD120-CD220 are redshifted with the CDs concentration gradually increases. Especially, CD200 and CD220 present PL emission peaks at around 600 nm. Meanwhile, compared with their respective diluted solution, the absorption spectra of the concentrated CD120-CD220 solutions are significantly broadened and shifted towards longer wavelengths (Fig. S8), demonstrating the formation of aggregation in the concentrated solution. The distance between nanoparticles will be shortened. One important factor for effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is the energy donor and acceptor must be close enough to increase the probability of energy transfer, and once the distance approaches Förster radius R0, FRET may occur [36]. Furthermore, the plots of (αhν)2 versus hν were obtained by converting the absorption spectra of CD120-CD220 to further determine their optical bandgaps (Eg), where α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, and light frequency, respectively, and Eg values are the corresponding intercepts on the x-axis (Fig. S9). From the plots, it is evident that as the CD concentration increases, the Eg values (i.e., intercepts on the x-axis) gradually decrease. When the concentration reaches 40 mg/mL, the Eg values of CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200, and CD220 decrease by 0.19, 0.224, 0.245, 0.315 and 1.827 eV, respectively, indicating that the influence of concentration on the optical bandgap from CD120 to CD220 is gradually strengthening (Table S3). These results manifest that the concentration-dependent redshift can attributed to FRET phenomenon [28,36,37].

Additionally, another important factor for effective energy transfer is the emission spectrum of the donor must overlap with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor [36]. To further illustrate the FRET phenomenon, the absorption spectra of the concentrated solution (40 mg/mL) were overlapped with the emission spectra of the diluted solution (0.5 mg/mL), and their spectral overlap was calculated to evaluate the FRET efficiency of these CDs. The overlap of CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200, and CD220 is 28.12 %, 44.70 %, 54.73 %, 57.74 %, and 91.35 %, respectively (Fig. 2i), proving the different FRET efficiency of these CDs, further resulting in the tunable solid-state fluorescence colors from blue to red. However, it is worth noting that only CD120 has an overlap of less than 30 % which does not meet the conditions of FRET [37]. This means that even with significant aggregation, the fluorescence color of CD120 remains similar to that in the dispersed state.

It is well known that CDs prefer to fluorescence quenching in aggregated state due to the consumption of transition energy by π-π conjugated stacking [21]. However, the prepared AIE CDs still maintain solid-state fluorescence (SSF). This may be mainly attributed to the S-S bonds, which enhance hydrophobicity and lead to aggregation, further limiting the intramolecular rotation (RIR) of the disulfide bond axis in axisymmetric heterocyclic compounds. Furthermore, the constrained motion or vibration of molecules on the aggregated surface of CDs can suppress non-radiative transitions in CDs, thereby greatly enhancing the surface-state fluorescence of CDs. Meanwhile, the emission of the carbon core is quenched due to excessive energy transfer through Förster resonance, achieving red-shifted SSF. On the contrary, moving molecules on the surface of dispersed CDs consume excessive non-radiative energy, leading to the extinction of surface radiation, ultimately presenting blue fluorescence emission from the carbon core (Fig. 2j) [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CD120, CD140, CD180, CD200 and CD220
ce:section.ce:para: To further illustrate the relationship between the structural composition and fluorescence mechanism of these CDs, the morphology and structure of the prepared CDs were analyzed in detailwith TEM, XPS, XRD, and FT-IR. CD140 and CD220 were characterized with TEM as representative samples. TEM images revealed that CD140 and CD220 are spherical particles, and high-resolution lattice stripe with a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm corresponds to the graphite (002) plane (Fig. 3 a and b) [38–40]. The corresponding size distributions of CD140 and CD220 predominantly range from 4 to 8 nm, and 6 to 11 nm, respectively, and the average particle sizes of CD140 and CD220 increase from 6.2 to 9.0 nm. Quantum size effect is considered one of the key factors governing the optical properties. Although the sizes of CDs were not uniform, the average size of the obtained CDs tends to increase with the increase of reaction temperature. Therefore, the different particle sizes may be account for the corresponding fluorescence emissions differences of CD120–CD220 [41,42].

Furthermore, the surface functional groups of CD120–CD220 were characterized using XPS. As shown in Fig. 3c, e, g, i, k, the XPS survey spectra indicate the presence of four elements (C, N, O and S) in the CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and their relative element content are presented in Fig. S10 and Table S4, confirming the presence of C-C, CC, CO/CN, and CO functionalitie [30,43,44]. The O 1 s XPS spectra are resolved into two peaks at 531.2 and 533.4 eV assigned to C-O and CO groups (Fig. S11) and the corresponding contents are provided in (Table S5) [43,45–47]. The N 1 s peaks at 399. 1 eV and 400.3 eV indicate the presence of nitrogen primarily in the form of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N (Fig. 3d, f, h, j, l) [30,46]. Remarkably, on the one hand, the nitrogen content gradually increases from 1.02 % to 1.87 % (Table S6). On the other hand, there is a significant difference in the proportion of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N, as shown in Table S7. With the increase of reaction temperature, the content of pyridinic-N increases from 19.50 % of CD120 to 87.86 % of CD220, while pyrrolic-N decreases from 80.50 % to 12.14 %. This suggests that the differences in nitrogen-based functional groups may be another key reason for the different optical properties of the five CDs [48]. Additionally, the S2p spectra in Fig. S12 includes three peaks corresponding to S-C, S-H, and S-S. It is worth noting that the data in Table S8 also show an increase in the proportion of S-S, from 7.72 % (CD120) to 13.32 % (CD220), which primarilyresponsiblefor the AIE of CDs. Based on the above analyses, it is inferred that the fluorescence emission variation of CD120–CD220 is mainly associated with the doped S and N. That is, nitrogen and sulfur play key roles for regulating the fluorescence emission of the synthesized CDs [49,50].

From XRD analysis, as shown in Fig. 3m, CD120–CD220 exhibit a distinct carbon peak at 2θ = 26°, indicating the presence of a graphitic carbon core. Remarkably, as-prepared CDs has a series of sharp diffraction peaks rather than the typical broad peaks around 26°, suggesting the good crystallization properties of CDs powder. Similar results are also observed in CDs synthesized in previous reports [51,52]. The CDs and the standard XRD data of DTSA crystals match well, indicating the crystals of the CDs as DTSA. This further confirms that CDs are mainly formed through a series of polymerization and carbonization reactions of DTSA during the solvent thermal process. Moreover, compared to the peaks observed 5-21°, the peaks gradually disappear with increasing temperature, indicating an increase in the degree of carbonization with temperature.

Analysis of FT-IR spectra, as shown in Fig. 3n, revealed the presence of functional groups or chemical bands such as amide carbonyl (1680 cm−1), S-H (2660 cm−1), CN (1680 cm−1), CC (1560 cm−1), CO (1410 cm−1), C-N (1270 cm−1), C-H (730 cm−1), C-S (698 cm−1), and S-S (550.0 cm−1) in these CDs samples [43,48]. Additionally, compared to DTSA, CDs exhibit weak absorption peaks at around 3050 cm−1, indicating a reduction in the content of hydrophilic groups of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups after amidation and carbonization. Correspondingly, this enhances the hydrophobicity of CDs to a certain extent [53].

Furthermore, based on the discoloration behavior of CD120–CD220 in aggregated and dispersed states. Chromotropic hydrogels were fabricated by dissolving CDs and agarose in DMSO, and then dropping the resulting solution into water. When the fabricated CD220 hydrogel was placed in water, the replacement of DMSO by water molecules as the solvent caused the CDs to initiate aggregation from the periphery towards the center of the gel. At the same time, the fluorescence color gradually changed from blue to rose red from the periphery to the center (Fig. 4 a and b). CD180 and CD200 hydrogels exhibited similar transformation processes (Fig. S13), only with the color change from blue to cyan and from blue to pink, respectively. It is noteworthy that the change processes are diffusion-controlled and triggered by the stimuli of water-glycol exchange. Moreover, this phenomenon was also observed using an upright fluorescence microscope (Fig. 4c). It revealed the initial aggregation of CDs at the edge of the gel, and the aggregation gradually diffused from the edge to the inside of the gel with time. Additionally, the PL emission spectra of the prepared hydrogels in water and ethanol were presented in Fig. S14. Clearly, the color of the gel before and afterdiscoloration in water and ethanol is approximately the same as that of CDs in dispersed and aggregated state, indicating that the color change of the gel is primarily governed by the CDs. It must be noted that the color change of CD120, CD140 and CD180 hydrogels is not pronounced, which may be due to the combination of the following factors. Firstly, the color changes of these three CDs themselves are not significant in aggregated and dispersed states. Besides, the network structure of the hydrogel further inhibits the dispersion and aggregation of CDs, resulting in less obvious color change of the gel.

In addition, the CDs were dissolved in ethanol to prepare ink and sprayed on maple leaf shaped filter paper, achieving maple leaves exhibiting blue fluorescence (Fig. 4d). Subsequently, when water was introduced, the maple leaves exhibited different luminous colors under 365 nm excitation due to the aggregation of CDs in water. Interestingly, after spraying ethanol again, the color returned to blue emission, realizing information encryption. Significantly, the color changes in CD120 and CD140 were less pronounced, for the same reason as in the discoloration gel.

Furthermore, three different types of models were designed, as shown in Fig. 4e: three-dimensional rose flower, two-dimensional rabbit, or assembled models five-petal flower. The rose model was fabricated with CD220, presenting the blue and red fluorescence immersed in ethanol and water, respectively. While the rabbit model was designed using CD180 (carrot tops), CD200 (carrot), and CD220 (rabbit).It was found that the fluorescence colors of rabbit model made of different CDs are difficult to distinguish in ethanol under the irradiation of UV 365 nm. Conversely, different color of each part in water formed a sharp contrast with that of in ethanol. Similarly, the five-petal flower model was designed using all five types of CDs. It is evident that color change can be achieved in different solvents. Given its remarkable color-changing performance, these models can be potentially applied for anti-counterfeiting purposes.

In addition, owing to their strong solid-state and lipophilic fluorescence properties, the prepared CDs are excellent phosphor powders for latent fingerprints detection on different substrates by dusting method. For the ease of research, CD180, CD200 and CD220 were utilized for fingerprint detection. After development on a glass sheet, due to the high contrast of the fluorescence signal, not only fingerprint images but also distinct features such as bifurcations, islands, terminations, loops, cores, ridges, and intersections could be clearly observed (Fig. 5 a) [48]. For comparison, the same fingerprints were also pressed on different materials, such as glass, phone case, plastic sheet, and metal surface (Fig. 5b), followed by the same development with the typical CD220. It is evident that fingerprints were clearly visible and the details of such latent fingerprints on different substrate were consistent, which confirms the reliability and repeatability of our CDs.

In summary, we successfully synthesized five different fluorescent colors of AIE CDs only by controlling the solvothermal temperature. In the presence of water, the hydrophobic CDs undergo continuous self-assembly, resulting in the quenching of short-wavelength emission due to the ACQ effect on the carbon cores, accompanied by the formation of long-wavelength AIE solid-state fluorescence. The difference in nitrogen and sulfur content and functional group ratio, as well as the size of CDs, synergistically induce different AIE behavior and FRET efficiency, leading to the red-shifted emission of CD120 to CD220. Finally, color-changing gel were fabricated using the color-changing property of CDs in dispersion and aggregation states, which can be further applied in anti-counterfeiting technology. Furthermore, owing to the lipophilic luminescent properties of CDs powder, our prepared AIE CDs displayed clearly the fine structure of fingerprints in fingerprint detection. These results demonstrate the potential of the prepared CDs in various fields.
Liming Fu: Writing – original draft. Cheng Zhang: Data curation. Haitao Li: Data curation. Ke Jian: Data curation. Yunhai Han: Data curation. Xihui Zhao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022ME159) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110765.
